global_nodes.label: Santa Fe Institute
global_nodes.description: A research institute dedicated to the study of complex systems, particularly in the context of chaos theory and network science
global_nodes.fun_fact: The Santa Fe Institute has been a hub for interdisciplinary research, bringing together scientists from fields like physics, biology, and economics

global_nodes.label: Community Detection
global_nodes.description: A technique used to identify clusters or communities within complex networks
global_nodes.fun_fact: Community detection has been used in fields like social network analysis, epidemiology, and marketing

global_nodes.label: Systems Thinking
global_nodes.description: An approach to understanding complex systems by analyzing the relationships and interactions between their components.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The concept of systems thinking has been around since the 1950s, but it gained popularity in the 1980s with the work of Peter Senge.

global_nodes.label: Holism
global_nodes.description: The idea that the whole is more than the sum of its parts, and that the relationships between components are just as important as the components themselves.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The term 'holism' was coined by Jan Smuts in 1926.

global_nodes.label: Feedback Loops
global_nodes.description: Cycles of cause-and-effect that drive the behavior of complex systems.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Feedback loops can be either positive (reinforcing) or negative (balancing).

global_nodes.label: Causal Loop Diagrams
global_nodes.description: A visual tool used to map the causal relationships between variables in a system.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Causal loop diagrams are often used in system dynamics modeling.

global_nodes.label: Stock and Flow Diagrams
global_nodes.description: A visual tool used to model the flow of resources through a system.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Stock and flow diagrams are often used in system dynamics modeling to understand the dynamics of complex systems.

global_nodes.label: System Dynamics
global_nodes.description: The study of the behavior of complex systems over time, using tools such as causal loop diagrams and stock and flow diagrams.
global_nodes.fun_fact: System dynamics was developed in the 1950s by Jay Forrester.

global_nodes.label: Jay Forrester
global_nodes.description: An American computer engineer and systems scientist who developed the field of system dynamics.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Forrester is known for his work on the development of the first computerized simulation model of a complex system.

global_nodes.label: Peter Senge
global_nodes.description: An American systems scientist and author who popularized the concept of systems thinking.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Senge is known for his book 'The Fifth Discipline', which introduced the concept of systems thinking to a wide audience.

global_nodes.label: The Fifth Discipline
global_nodes.description: A book by Peter Senge that introduces the concept of systems thinking and its application to organizational learning.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The book has been translated into over 20 languages and has sold over 1 million copies worldwide.

global_nodes.label: Autonomous Driving Systems
global_nodes.description: A complex system that uses sensors, software, and hardware to enable vehicles to drive themselves.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Autonomous driving systems use a combination of machine learning, computer vision, and sensor data to navigate roads and avoid obstacles.

global_nodes.label: Simulation-Based Testing
global_nodes.description: A method of testing complex systems using simulated scenarios and environments.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Simulation-based testing is often used in the development of autonomous driving systems to test and validate their performance.

global_nodes.label: Safety-Aware Mutation Testing
global_nodes.description: A method of testing complex systems that involves introducing faults or mutations into the system and evaluating its response.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Safety-aware mutation testing is often used in the development of autonomous driving systems to test and validate their safety and reliability.

global_nodes.label: Engineering a Safer World
global_nodes.description: A book by Nancy Leveson that applies systems thinking to the field of safety engineering.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The book introduces a new approach to safety engineering that focuses on understanding the complex interactions between system components.

global_nodes.label: Nancy Leveson
global_nodes.description: An American systems scientist and author who has made significant contributions to the field of safety engineering.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Leveson is known for her work on the development of the STAMP (Systems-Theoretic Accident Model and Processes) framework for safety engineering.

global_nodes.label: STAMP Framework
global_nodes.description: A framework for safety engineering that focuses on understanding the complex interactions between system components.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The STAMP framework has been applied to a wide range of complex systems, including autonomous driving systems and healthcare systems.

global_nodes.label: Public Health
global_nodes.description: The science of preventing disease, prolonging life, and promoting health through organized efforts and informed choices.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The concept of public health has been around since ancient civilizations, with evidence of public health practices dating back to ancient Greece and Rome.

global_nodes.label: Epidemiology
global_nodes.description: The study of the distribution and determinants of health-related events, diseases, or health-related characteristics among populations.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The term 'epidemiology' was first used in the 19th century to describe the study of epidemics, but it has since expanded to include the study of all types of health-related events.

global_nodes.label: Infectious Disease Control
global_nodes.description: The practice of preventing and controlling the spread of infectious diseases, including through vaccination, quarantine, and contact tracing.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The discovery of the germ theory of disease by Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch in the 19th century revolutionized the field of infectious disease control.

global_nodes.label: Health Policy
global_nodes.description: The plans, policies, and decisions made by governments and other organizations to promote and protect the health of populations.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The World Health Organization (WHO) plays a key role in shaping global health policy, including through the development of international health regulations and guidelines.

global_nodes.label: Global Health Disparities
global_nodes.description: The differences in health outcomes and access to healthcare between different populations, including between rich and poor countries, and within countries between different socioeconomic groups.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The WHO has identified the reduction of health disparities as one of its key priorities, and has developed a range of initiatives and programs to address this issue.

global_nodes.label: Healthcare Systems
global_nodes.description: The organizations, institutions, and resources that provide healthcare services to populations, including hospitals, clinics, and healthcare providers.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The United States has a mixed healthcare system, with both public and private providers, while many other countries have single-payer or socialized healthcare systems.

global_nodes.label: John Snow
global_nodes.description: A British physician who is considered one of the founders of modern epidemiology, known for his work on cholera outbreaks in London in the 19th century.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Snow's work on cholera led to the identification of the source of the outbreak as a contaminated water pump, and his findings were instrumental in the development of modern water treatment systems.

global_nodes.label: Louis Pasteur
global_nodes.description: A French chemist and microbiologist who developed the germ theory of disease and made significant contributions to the field of infectious disease control.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Pasteur developed the first vaccines for rabies and anthrax, and his work on germ theory led to the development of modern vaccines and antibiotics.

global_nodes.label: World Health Organization (WHO)
global_nodes.description: A specialized agency of the United Nations that is responsible for international public health, including the development of global health policies and guidelines.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The WHO was established in 1948 and is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, with 194 member states and a budget of over $4 billion.

global_nodes.label: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
global_nodes.description: A US federal agency that is responsible for protecting the public's health and safety, including through the prevention and control of infectious diseases.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The CDC was established in 1946 and is headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia, with a budget of over $7 billion and a staff of over 12,000 people.

global_nodes.label: Vaccination
global_nodes.description: The practice of administering vaccines to prevent infectious diseases, including through routine childhood immunizations and outbreak response.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Vaccination has been shown to be one of the most effective ways to prevent infectious diseases, with estimates suggesting that it has saved millions of lives worldwide.

global_nodes.label: Contact Tracing
global_nodes.description: The practice of identifying and monitoring individuals who have come into contact with someone who has an infectious disease, in order to prevent further transmission.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Contact tracing has been used to control outbreaks of diseases such as Ebola and SARS, and is a key component of infectious disease control efforts.

global_nodes.label: Health Promotion
global_nodes.description: The practice of promoting healthy behaviors and lifestyles, including through education, community outreach, and policy change.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Health promotion has been shown to be an effective way to prevent chronic diseases, including through the promotion of physical activity, healthy eating, and tobacco cessation.

global_nodes.label: Global Health Security
global_nodes.description: The protection of people from health threats, including infectious diseases, natural disasters, and other crises, through the development of robust health systems and emergency response capabilities.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The WHO has developed a range of initiatives and programs to strengthen global health security, including the International Health Regulations and the Global Health Security Agenda.

global_nodes.label: Health Equity
global_nodes.description: The principle of promoting fairness and justice in the distribution of health resources and outcomes, including through the reduction of health disparities and the promotion of health for all.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The WHO has identified health equity as a key priority, and has developed a range of initiatives and programs to promote health equity, including the Health Equity Framework.

global_nodes.label: Cryptography
global_nodes.description: The practice and study of techniques for secure communication in the presence of adversarial behavior.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The word 'cryptography' comes from the Greek words 'kryptos' meaning 'hidden' and 'graphein' meaning 'writing'.

global_nodes.label: Encryption Algorithms
global_nodes.description: Algorithms used to transform plaintext into ciphertext to protect data from unauthorized access.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The first encryption algorithm was the Caesar Cipher, which was used by Julius Caesar to send secret messages.

global_nodes.label: Digital Signatures
global_nodes.description: A type of asymmetric cryptography that uses a pair of keys to authenticate the sender of a message and ensure the integrity of the message.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Digital signatures are used in electronic commerce to authenticate transactions and ensure the integrity of data.

global_nodes.label: Cryptanalysis
global_nodes.description: The study of methods for obtaining the meaning of encrypted information without access to the decryption key.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Cryptanalysis was used during World War II to break the German Enigma code.

global_nodes.label: Hash Functions
global_nodes.description: Algorithms that take input data of any size and produce a fixed-size string of characters, known as a hash value or digest.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Hash functions are used in data storage and retrieval to quickly identify and verify data.

global_nodes.label: Secure Communication Protocols
global_nodes.description: Protocols used to ensure the secure transmission of data over a network.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Secure communication protocols such as HTTPS and SSH are used to protect data in transit.

global_nodes.label: Elliptic Curve Cryptography
global_nodes.description: A type of public-key cryptography that uses the mathematics of elliptic curves to create a secure key exchange.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Elliptic curve cryptography is used in many modern cryptographic protocols due to its high security and efficiency.

global_nodes.label: RSA Algorithm
global_nodes.description: A widely used public-key encryption algorithm that uses the principles of number theory to create a secure key exchange.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The RSA algorithm was developed in 1978 by Ron Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman.

global_nodes.label: AES Algorithm
global_nodes.description: A symmetric-key encryption algorithm that is widely used to protect data at rest and in transit.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The AES algorithm was developed in 2001 by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST).

global_nodes.label: Quantum Cryptography
global_nodes.description: A type of cryptography that uses the principles of quantum mechanics to create secure key exchange.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Quantum cryptography is still in its early stages of development, but it has the potential to provide unbreakable encryption.

global_nodes.label: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange
global_nodes.description: A key exchange algorithm that allows two parties to establish a shared secret key over an insecure channel.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The Diffie-Hellman key exchange was developed in 1976 by Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman.

global_nodes.label: Electronic Commerce
global_nodes.description: The use of cryptography to secure online transactions and protect sensitive data.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Electronic commerce relies heavily on cryptography to protect transactions and maintain customer trust.

global_nodes.label: Chip-Based Payment Cards
global_nodes.description: The use of cryptography to secure payment card transactions and protect sensitive data.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Chip-based payment cards use cryptography to protect transactions and prevent unauthorized access to sensitive data.

global_nodes.label: Digital Currencies
global_nodes.description: The use of cryptography to secure and verify transactions in digital currencies such as Bitcoin.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Digital currencies rely heavily on cryptography to secure and verify transactions and maintain the integrity of the blockchain.

global_nodes.label: Computer Passwords
global_nodes.description: The use of cryptography to protect and verify computer passwords.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Computer passwords are protected using cryptography to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive data.

global_nodes.label: Military Communications
global_nodes.description: The use of cryptography to secure military communications and protect sensitive data.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Military communications rely heavily on cryptography to protect sensitive data and maintain national security.

global_nodes.label: Cartography
global_nodes.description: The science and practice of creating maps, involving the compilation, analysis, and representation of geographic data.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The word 'cartography' comes from the Greek words 'carta' meaning map and 'graphy' meaning writing.

global_nodes.label: Geographic Information Systems
global_nodes.description: A computer-based tool for capturing, storing, analyzing, and displaying geographically referenced data.
global_nodes.fun_fact: GIS technology is used in a wide range of applications, including urban planning, emergency response, and natural resource management.

global_nodes.label: Map Projections
global_nodes.description: A way of representing the curved surface of the Earth on a flat map, using mathematical formulas to preserve certain properties such as shape or size.
global_nodes.fun_fact: There are many different types of map projections, each with its own strengths and weaknesses, and the choice of projection depends on the purpose of the map.

global_nodes.label: Spatial Analysis
global_nodes.description: A set of techniques used to analyze and understand the relationships between geographic phenomena, using statistical and mathematical methods.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Spatial analysis is used in a wide range of fields, including epidemiology, criminology, and environmental science.

global_nodes.label: Remote Sensing
global_nodes.description: The use of sensors to collect data about the Earth's surface, without being in direct physical contact with it, often using satellite or airborne platforms.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Remote sensing is used to monitor environmental changes, such as deforestation, ocean currents, and climate patterns.

global_nodes.label: Historical Cartography
global_nodes.description: The study of the history of cartography, including the development of mapping techniques, the evolution of map-making tools, and the impact of cartography on society.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Historical cartography can provide insights into the cultural, social, and political context of past societies.

global_nodes.label: John Snyder
global_nodes.description: An American cartographer who developed the Snyder equal area map projection, which is used to preserve the area of features on the map.
global_nodes.fun_fact: John Snyder is known for his work on map projections and his contributions to the field of cartography.

global_nodes.label: Gerardus Mercator
global_nodes.description: A Flemish cartographer who developed the Mercator map projection, which is still widely used today for navigation and other purposes.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Gerardus Mercator is considered one of the most important cartographers in history, and his map projection has had a lasting impact on the field.

global_nodes.label: Urban Planning
global_nodes.description: The use of cartography and geographic information systems to plan and manage urban development, including the design of transportation systems, parks, and other infrastructure.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Urban planning is critical for creating sustainable, livable, and equitable cities, and cartography plays a key role in this process.

global_nodes.label: Environmental Monitoring
global_nodes.description: The use of cartography and remote sensing to monitor environmental changes, such as deforestation, ocean currents, and climate patterns.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Environmental monitoring is critical for understanding and mitigating the impacts of human activity on the environment, and cartography plays a key role in this process.

global_nodes.label: Navigation
global_nodes.description: The use of cartography and geographic information systems to navigate and orient oneself in space, including the use of GPS and other technologies.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Navigation is a critical component of many industries, including aviation, maritime, and logistics, and cartography plays a key role in this process.

global_nodes.label: Cartographic Communication
global_nodes.description: The process of conveying geographic information through maps, including the use of symbols, colors, and other visual elements to communicate meaning.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Cartographic communication is a critical component of cartography, and is used to convey complex geographic information to a wide range of audiences.

global_nodes.label: Map Reading
global_nodes.description: The process of interpreting and understanding the information presented on a map, including the use of legends, scales, and other cartographic elements.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Map reading is a critical skill for anyone who uses maps, and is essential for navigating and understanding geographic information.

global_nodes.label: Cartography in Education
global_nodes.description: The use of cartography and geographic information systems in educational settings, including the teaching of geography, environmental science, and other subjects.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Cartography is a powerful tool for teaching geographic concepts and skills, and is used in a wide range of educational settings.

global_nodes.label: Cartographic Design
global_nodes.description: The process of creating visually appealing and effective maps, including the use of color, typography, and other visual elements to communicate meaning.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Cartographic design is a critical component of cartography, and is used to create maps that are both informative and aesthetically pleasing.

global_nodes.label: Archaeoastronomy
global_nodes.description: The study of how people in the past understood and used astronomical phenomena, and what role the sky played in their cultures.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The term 'archaeoastronomy' was coined in the 1970s to describe this interdisciplinary field.

global_nodes.label: Ancient Astronomical Observations
global_nodes.description: The study of how ancient cultures observed and recorded celestial events, such as planetary alignments and solar eclipses.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ancient cultures developed sophisticated astronomical observation techniques, including the use of sundials and astronomical clocks.

global_nodes.label: Cultural Astronomy
global_nodes.description: The study of the cultural and symbolic significance of celestial events in ancient cultures, including their myths, legends, and rituals.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Many ancient cultures believed that celestial events had spiritual or mystical significance, and developed elaborate rituals to mark these events.

global_nodes.label: Astronomical Alignments
global_nodes.description: The study of how ancient cultures aligned their buildings, monuments, and other structures with celestial events, such as the solstices and equinoxes.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Many ancient structures, such as Stonehenge, are aligned with celestial events, demonstrating a sophisticated understanding of astronomy.

global_nodes.label: Ethnoastronomy
global_nodes.description: The study of how contemporary cultures understand and use astronomical phenomena, including their myths, legends, and rituals.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ethnoastronomy provides insights into the cultural and symbolic significance of celestial events in contemporary cultures, and highlights the importance of preserving traditional knowledge systems.

global_nodes.label: Historical Astronomy
global_nodes.description: The study of the history of astronomy, including the development of astronomical theories, instruments, and practices.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Historical astronomy provides insights into the development of modern astronomy, and highlights the contributions of ancient cultures to our understanding of the universe.

global_nodes.label: Clive Ruggles
global_nodes.description: A British archaeoastronomer who has made significant contributions to the field, including the development of new methods for analyzing ancient astronomical observations.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ruggles is a leading expert on the archaeoastronomy of ancient Britain, and has written extensively on the subject.

global_nodes.label: Schaefer
global_nodes.description: An American astronomer who has made significant contributions to the field of archaeoastronomy, including the development of new methods for analyzing ancient astronomical observations.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Schaefer is a leading expert on the archaeoastronomy of ancient Mesopotamia, and has written extensively on the subject.

global_nodes.label: Aveni
global_nodes.description: An American archaeoastronomer who has made significant contributions to the field, including the development of new methods for analyzing ancient astronomical observations.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Aveni is a leading expert on the archaeoastronomy of ancient Mesoamerica, and has written extensively on the subject.

global_nodes.label: Tomnaverie Recumbent Stone Circle
global_nodes.description: A ancient stone circle in Scotland that is aligned with celestial events, such as the solstices and equinoxes.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The Tomnaverie Recumbent Stone Circle is one of the most well-preserved ancient stone circles in Scotland, and provides insights into the astronomical knowledge of ancient cultures.

global_nodes.label: Stonehenge
global_nodes.description: A ancient monument in England that is aligned with celestial events, such as the solstices and equinoxes.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Stonehenge is one of the most famous ancient monuments in the world, and provides insights into the astronomical knowledge of ancient cultures.

global_nodes.label: Interpretative Archaeoastronomy
global_nodes.description: A approach to archaeoastronomy that emphasizes the interpretation of ancient astronomical observations in their cultural and historical context.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Interpretative archaeoastronomy provides insights into the cultural and symbolic significance of celestial events in ancient cultures, and highlights the importance of preserving traditional knowledge systems.

global_nodes.label: Methodological Issues
global_nodes.description: The study of the methods and techniques used in archaeoastronomy, including the analysis of ancient astronomical observations and the interpretation of cultural and historical context.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Methodological issues are a key area of debate in archaeoastronomy, with different approaches and techniques being developed to analyze ancient astronomical observations.

global_nodes.label: Archaeoastronomy and Anthropology
global_nodes.description: The study of the relationship between archaeoastronomy and anthropology, including the use of anthropological theories and methods to interpret ancient astronomical observations.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The relationship between archaeoastronomy and anthropology is a key area of research, with anthropological theories and methods being used to interpret ancient astronomical observations.

global_nodes.label: Archaeoastronomy and History
global_nodes.description: The study of the relationship between archaeoastronomy and history, including the use of historical records to interpret ancient astronomical observations.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The relationship between archaeoastronomy and history is a key area of research, with historical records being used to interpret ancient astronomical observations.

global_nodes.label: Ethnobotany
global_nodes.description: The study of the relationships between humans and plants, focusing on traditional knowledge of plant use, management, and perception in human societies.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ethnobotany is an interdisciplinary field that combines botany, anthropology, ecology, and chemistry.

global_nodes.label: Plant Systematics
global_nodes.description: The study of the identification, classification, and naming of plants.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Plant systematics is a fundamental concept in ethnobotany, as it helps researchers understand the diversity of plant species used by different cultures.

global_nodes.label: Traditional Medicine
global_nodes.description: The use of plants and other natural products to prevent, diagnose, and treat diseases.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Traditional medicine is one of the most significant applications of ethnobotany, as many modern medicines are derived from plants used in traditional medicine.

global_nodes.label: Botanical Anthropology
global_nodes.description: The study of the cultural significance of plants in human societies.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Botanical anthropology is a key concept in ethnobotany, as it helps researchers understand the social and cultural context of plant use.

global_nodes.label: Phytochemistry
global_nodes.description: The study of the chemical composition of plants and their potential medicinal properties.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Phytochemistry is a crucial concept in ethnobotany, as it helps researchers understand the chemical basis of plant-based medicines.

global_nodes.label: Conservation Biology
global_nodes.description: The study of the conservation and management of plant species and ecosystems.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Conservation biology is a significant application of ethnobotany, as it helps researchers develop strategies for preserving plant diversity and promoting sustainable use of plant resources.

global_nodes.label: Richard Evans Schultes
global_nodes.description: An American botanist and ethnobotanist who is considered the 'father of ethnobotany'.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Schultes is credited with coining the term 'ethnobotany' and establishing the field as a distinct area of study.

global_nodes.label: Khevsureti, Republic of Georgia
global_nodes.description: A region in the Caucasus where ethnobotanical research has been conducted to study the traditional use of plants by local communities.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The Khevsureti region is home to a diverse range of plant species and has a rich cultural heritage of traditional plant use.

global_nodes.label: Samburu People, Kenya
global_nodes.description: An African community that has been studied for their traditional use of plants, including medicinal plants and food crops.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The Samburu people have a deep understanding of the local flora and have developed a range of traditional plant-based remedies for various ailments.

global_nodes.label: Importance Indices
global_nodes.description: A method used in ethnobotany to quantify the importance of plants and vegetation to people.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Importance indices are used to analyze the cultural significance of plants and to identify priority areas for conservation and management.

global_nodes.label: Caucasus Biodiversity Hotspot
global_nodes.description: A region of high plant diversity and endemism, with a rich cultural heritage of traditional plant use.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The Caucasus biodiversity hotspot is home to over 6,000 plant species, many of which are found nowhere else in the world.

global_nodes.label: Traditional Plant Use
global_nodes.description: The use of plants by local communities for various purposes, including medicine, food, and religious use.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Traditional plant use is a key aspect of ethnobotany, as it reflects the cultural significance of plants in human societies.

global_nodes.label: Plant-Related Customs
global_nodes.description: The cultural practices and traditions associated with plant use, including rituals, ceremonies, and taboos.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Plant-related customs are an important aspect of ethnobotany, as they reflect the social and cultural context of plant use.

global_nodes.label: Ethnobotanical Research
global_nodes.description: The study of the relationships between humans and plants, including the documentation and analysis of traditional plant use.

global_nodes.label: Earthquake Prediction

global_nodes.label: Demography
global_nodes.description: The study of the size, structure, and distribution of human populations

global_nodes.label: Population Dynamics
global_nodes.description: The study of the changes in population size and structure over time
global_nodes.fun_fact: Population dynamics is influenced by factors such as fertility, mortality, and migration

global_nodes.label: Migration Patterns
global_nodes.description: The study of the movement of people from one place to another
global_nodes.fun_fact: Migration patterns can be influenced by factors such as economic opportunities, war, and environmental disasters

global_nodes.label: Fertility Rates
global_nodes.description: The study of the number of births per 1,000 people per year
global_nodes.fun_fact: Fertility rates are influenced by factors such as education, income, and access to family planning resources

global_nodes.label: Mortality Rates
global_nodes.description: The study of the number of deaths per 1,000 people per year
global_nodes.fun_fact: Mortality rates are influenced by factors such as healthcare access, lifestyle, and environmental factors

global_nodes.label: Urbanization
global_nodes.description: The process of people moving from rural areas to cities
global_nodes.fun_fact: Urbanization can lead to changes in population dynamics, including increased fertility rates and mortality rates

global_nodes.label: Thomas Malthus
global_nodes.description: An English economist and demographer who wrote about population growth and its effects on society
global_nodes.fun_fact: Malthus is known for his theory that population growth will eventually outstrip food supply, leading to poverty and famine

global_nodes.label: Demographic Transition
global_nodes.description: The process by which a society's population changes from high birth and death rates to low birth and death rates
global_nodes.fun_fact: The demographic transition is often associated with economic development and improvements in healthcare

global_nodes.label: Population Pyramid
global_nodes.description: A graphical representation of a population's age and sex structure
global_nodes.fun_fact: Population pyramids can be used to predict future population trends and plan public services

global_nodes.label: Census Data
global_nodes.description: Data collected by governments to understand population size, structure, and distribution
global_nodes.fun_fact: Census data is used to inform policy decisions and allocate resources

global_nodes.label: Population Growth Rate
global_nodes.description: The rate at which a population is growing or declining
global_nodes.fun_fact: The population growth rate is calculated using the formula: (births - deaths + migration) / total population

global_nodes.label: Aging Population
global_nodes.description: A population with a high proportion of older adults
global_nodes.fun_fact: Aging populations can lead to changes in population dynamics, including increased healthcare costs and pension liabilities

global_nodes.label: Youth Bulge
global_nodes.description: A population with a high proportion of young people
global_nodes.fun_fact: Youth bulges can lead to changes in population dynamics, including increased economic growth and social unrest

global_nodes.label: Demographic Analysis
global_nodes.description: The study of population data to understand trends and patterns
global_nodes.fun_fact: Demographic analysis is used in a variety of fields, including marketing, healthcare, and urban planning

global_nodes.label: United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs
global_nodes.description: A organization that provides demographic data and analysis to inform policy decisions
global_nodes.fun_fact: The UN DESA is a leading source of demographic data and analysis

global_nodes.label: Epidemiology
global_nodes.description: The study and analysis of the distribution, patterns, and determinants of health and disease conditions in a defined population.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The term 'epidemiology' comes from the Greek words 'epi' meaning 'upon', 'demos' meaning 'people', and 'logos' meaning 'study'.

global_nodes.label: Infectious Disease Control
global_nodes.description: The strategies and methods used to prevent and control the spread of infectious diseases.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Vaccination is one of the most effective ways to control infectious diseases.

global_nodes.label: Statistical Analysis of Health Data
global_nodes.description: The use of statistical methods to analyze and interpret health data, including disease trends and risk factors.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Statistical analysis is crucial in identifying disease outbreaks and understanding the effectiveness of interventions.

global_nodes.label: Disease Outbreak Investigation
global_nodes.description: The process of identifying and investigating disease outbreaks to determine their causes and control measures.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Disease outbreak investigations often involve a multidisciplinary team of epidemiologists, laboratory scientists, and healthcare professionals.

global_nodes.label: Vaccine Development
global_nodes.description: The process of designing, testing, and implementing vaccines to prevent infectious diseases.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Vaccines have been instrumental in controlling and eliminating many infectious diseases, including smallpox and polio.

global_nodes.label: Global Health Policy
global_nodes.description: The policies and guidelines that govern global health initiatives and programs, including disease control and prevention.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Global health policy is shaped by international organizations, governments, and non-governmental organizations.

global_nodes.label: John Snow
global_nodes.description: A British physician who is considered one of the founders of modern epidemiology, known for his work on cholera outbreaks.
global_nodes.fun_fact: John Snow's work on cholera led to the removal of a contaminated water pump in London, which helped to control the outbreak.

global_nodes.label: Cholera
global_nodes.description: A highly infectious disease caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae, often spread through contaminated water or food.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Cholera outbreaks have been responsible for millions of deaths throughout history, but vaccination and improved sanitation have reduced the disease burden.

global_nodes.label: SARS-CoV-2
global_nodes.description: The virus responsible for the COVID-19 pandemic, which has had a significant impact on global health and economies.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The rapid development and distribution of COVID-19 vaccines have been crucial in controlling the pandemic.

global_nodes.label: Contact Tracing
global_nodes.description: The process of identifying and monitoring individuals who have come into contact with someone infected with a disease, to prevent further transmission.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Contact tracing is a critical component of disease outbreak investigation and control.

global_nodes.label: Epidemiological Study Designs
global_nodes.description: The methods used to design and conduct epidemiological studies, including case-control, cohort, and cross-sectional studies.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Epidemiological study designs are essential for understanding disease causes and risk factors.

global_nodes.label: Surveillance
global_nodes.description: The ongoing, systematic collection, analysis, and interpretation of health data to monitor disease trends and detect outbreaks.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Surveillance is critical for early detection and response to disease outbreaks.

global_nodes.label: World Health Organization (WHO)

global_nodes.fun_fact: The WHO plays a crucial role in coordinating global health initiatives and providing technical assistance to countries.

global_nodes.label: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
global_nodes.description: A US federal agency responsible for protecting public health and safety through disease control and prevention, including epidemiological investigations.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The CDC is a leading authority on infectious disease control and prevention, and provides guidance and support to healthcare professionals and governments.

global_nodes.label: Epidemiological Modeling
global_nodes.description: The use of mathematical models to simulate disease transmission and predict the impact of interventions.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Epidemiological modeling is essential for informing public health policy and decision-making.

global_nodes.label: Archaeology

global_nodes.fun_fact: The word 'archaeology' comes from the Greek words 'archaios' (ancient) and 'logos' (study).

global_nodes.label: Cultural Resource Management
global_nodes.description: The practice of managing and preserving cultural resources, such as historical sites and artifacts.

global_nodes.label: Historical Preservation

global_nodes.fun_fact: Historical preservation is essential for understanding the past and learning from it.

global_nodes.label: Anthropological Theory

global_nodes.label: Museum Studies

global_nodes.label: Cultural Heritage Policy

global_nodes.label: Excavation

global_nodes.label: Surveying

global_nodes.label: Archaeological Record

global_nodes.label: GIS Mapping

global_nodes.label: Location-Based Services

global_nodes.label: Natural Resource Management

global_nodes.label: GIS Software

global_nodes.label: GIS History

global_nodes.label: Biomechanical Engineering
global_nodes.description: A multidisciplinary field that combines principles of physics, biology, and engineering to understand the mechanical aspects of living systems.

global_nodes.description: Materials used to develop medical devices and implants that interact with living tissues.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Biomaterials can be derived from natural sources, such as collagen, or synthetic sources, such as titanium.

global_nodes.label: Tissue Engineering
global_nodes.description: A field that focuses on developing functional tissue substitutes to repair or replace damaged tissues.

global_nodes.label: Orthopedic Implants
global_nodes.description: Medical devices used to replace or repair damaged joints, bones, and muscles.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Orthopedic implants can be made from a variety of biomaterials, including titanium, stainless steel, and ceramic.

global_nodes.label: Rehabilitation Robotics

global_nodes.fun_fact: Rehabilitation robots can provide personalized therapy and feedback to patients, improving their recovery outcomes.

global_nodes.label: Computational Modeling of Human Movement
global_nodes.description: The use of mathematical models and computer simulations to understand and predict human movement patterns.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Computational models of human movement can be used to develop personalized rehabilitation plans and optimize athletic performance.

global_nodes.label: Y.C. Fung
global_nodes.description: A renowned biomechanical engineer who made significant contributions to the field of biomechanics.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Y.C. Fung is known as the 'father of biomechanics' for his pioneering work in the field.

global_nodes.label: Biomechanics
global_nodes.description: The study of the mechanical aspects of living systems, including the structure, function, and movement of living organisms.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Biomechanics is an interdisciplinary field that combines principles from biology, physics, and engineering.

global_nodes.label: Regenerative Medicine
global_nodes.description: A field that focuses on developing innovative therapies to repair or replace damaged tissues and organs.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Regenerative medicine involves the use of biomaterials, stem cells, and growth factors to develop functional tissue substitutes.

global_nodes.label: Collagen
global_nodes.description: A type of biomaterial used in tissue engineering and regenerative medicine.

global_nodes.label: Tissue Growth

global_nodes.label: Prosthetic Limbs

global_nodes.label: Biomechanical Modeling

global_nodes.fun_fact: Biomechanical modeling can be used to develop personalized medicine and optimize treatment outcomes.

global_nodes.label: Medical Devices
