global_nodes.fun_fact: The term 'paleoclimatology' was first used in the 1960s to describe this field of study.

global_nodes.label: Ice Age Dynamics
global_nodes.description: The study of the formation, growth, and decay of ice ages, including the factors that influence ice sheet expansion and contraction.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The most recent ice age, which ended about 11,700 years ago, was characterized by large ice sheets covering much of North America and Europe.

global_nodes.label: Sea Level Changes
global_nodes.description: The study of changes in sea level over time, including the factors that contribute to these changes, such as ice sheet melting and ocean thermal expansion.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Sea levels have risen by about 20 cm since 1880, with an accelerating trend in recent decades.

global_nodes.label: Glacial Geology
global_nodes.description: The study of the geological features and landforms created by glaciers, including glacial erratics, moraines, and drumlins.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Glaciers have carved out many of the world's most spectacular landscapes, including the fjords of Norway and the mountains of New Zealand.

global_nodes.label: Climate Modeling
global_nodes.description: The use of computer models to simulate the Earth's climate system, including the interactions between atmosphere, oceans, land, and ice.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Climate models are used to predict future climate changes and to understand the impacts of different greenhouse gas emission scenarios.

global_nodes.label: Proxy Data Analysis
global_nodes.description: The analysis of indirect evidence of past climate conditions, such as tree rings, coral reefs, and ice cores, to reconstruct ancient climates.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Proxy data analysis has revealed that the Earth's climate has varied significantly over the past few thousand years, with periods of warming and cooling.

global_nodes.label: KNMI Climate Explorer
global_nodes.description: A web-based research tool for high-resolution paleoclimatology, providing access to a comprehensive collection of climatic data sets and analysis tools.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The KNMI Climate Explorer is managed by the Royal Netherlands Meteorological Institute and is widely used by researchers in the field of paleoclimatology.

global_nodes.label: Proxy System Modeling
global_nodes.description: The use of models to simulate the processes by which proxy data are formed, including the response of sensors to environmental forcing.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Proxy system modeling helps to improve the interpretation of proxy data and to reduce uncertainties in paleoclimate reconstructions.

global_nodes.label: Encyclopedia of Paleoclimatology and Ancient Environments
global_nodes.description: A comprehensive reference work covering all aspects of paleoclimatology, including the methods, techniques, and applications of the field.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The encyclopedia is a valuable resource for researchers, students, and professionals working in the field of paleoclimatology.

global_nodes.label: Louis Agassiz
global_nodes.description: A Swiss-American geologist and paleontologist who made significant contributions to the field of paleoclimatology, including the discovery of evidence for past ice ages.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Agassiz was one of the first scientists to propose the idea of an ice age, and his work laid the foundation for modern paleoclimatology.

global_nodes.label: Milutin Milankovic
global_nodes.description: A Serbian mathematician and engineer who developed the theory of ice age cycles, including the role of changes in Earth's orbit in driving climate variations.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Milankovic's work on ice age cycles is still widely used today in paleoclimatology and climate science.

global_nodes.label: Ice Core Drilling
global_nodes.description: A method of collecting ice cores from glaciers and ice sheets, which provide a record of past climate conditions, including temperature, precipitation, and atmospheric composition.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ice core drilling has revealed a detailed record of climate variations over the past few hundred thousand years, including the presence of greenhouse gases and other climate forcings.

global_nodes.label: Dendrochronology
global_nodes.description: The study of tree rings as a proxy for past climate conditions, including temperature, precipitation, and drought.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Dendrochronology has been used to reconstruct climate conditions over the past few thousand years, including the presence of droughts, floods, and other extreme events.

global_nodes.label: Climate Change Impacts
global_nodes.description: The study of the potential impacts of climate change on ecosystems, human health, and the economy, including the development of adaptation and mitigation strategies.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Climate change impacts are already being felt around the world, from more frequent natural disasters to changes in the distribution of plants and animals.

global_nodes.label: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC)
global_nodes.description: An international organization that provides scientific advice to policymakers on climate change, including the potential impacts and mitigation strategies.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The IPCC has played a key role in raising awareness about climate change and promoting international cooperation on climate action.

global_nodes.label: Oceanography
global_nodes.description: The scientific study of the ocean, including its physics, chemistry, biology, and geology.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The word 'oceanography' was first used in the 16th century.

global_nodes.label: Marine Geology
global_nodes.description: The study of the Earth's oceanic crust, including its composition, structure, and processes.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Marine geology helps us understand the formation of oceanic crust and the distribution of marine resources.

global_nodes.label: Physical Oceanography
global_nodes.description: The study of the ocean's physical properties, including its temperature, salinity, and circulation patterns.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Physical oceanography helps us understand ocean currents, waves, and the ocean's role in climate regulation.

global_nodes.label: Chemical Oceanography
global_nodes.description: The study of the ocean's chemical composition, including its nutrients, gases, and pollutants.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Chemical oceanography helps us understand the ocean's biogeochemical cycles and the impact of human activities on the ocean's chemistry.

global_nodes.label: Biological Oceanography
global_nodes.description: The study of the ocean's living organisms, including their diversity, distribution, and interactions.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Biological oceanography helps us understand the ocean's ecosystems, including coral reefs, phytoplankton, and marine food webs.

global_nodes.label: Ocean Conservation
global_nodes.description: The practice of protecting the ocean's ecosystems and resources from human impacts, including pollution, overfishing, and climate change.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ocean conservation efforts include the establishment of marine protected areas, sustainable fishing practices, and reduction of plastic pollution.

global_nodes.label: Matthew Fontaine Maury
global_nodes.description: An American oceanographer who is considered the 'father of modern oceanography' for his pioneering work on ocean currents and navigation.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Maury's work on ocean currents helped establish the United States as a major maritime power.

global_nodes.label: Marine Debris
global_nodes.description: A significant environmental problem that affects marine ecosystems, including plastic pollution, oil spills, and abandoned fishing gear.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Marine debris can harm marine life, contaminate the food chain, and damage coastal ecosystems.

global_nodes.label: Ocean Acidification
global_nodes.description: The decrease in the ocean's pH levels due to the absorption of excess carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, which can harm marine organisms, especially those with calcium carbonate shells.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ocean acidification is a major concern for coral reefs, shellfish, and other marine ecosystems.

global_nodes.label: El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO)
global_nodes.description: A complex climate phenomenon that affects the ocean's temperature, atmospheric circulation, and weather patterns, leading to droughts, floods, and other extreme weather events.
global_nodes.fun_fact: ENSO has significant impacts on global climate patterns, including precipitation, temperature, and sea level.

global_nodes.label: Southeast Asian Waters
global_nodes.description: A region of significant oceanographic interest, with unique physical and biological characteristics, including coral reefs, mangroves, and seagrass beds.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The Southeast Asian Waters are home to a diverse array of marine life, including endangered species such as sea turtles and dugongs.

global_nodes.label: Floating Marine Debris
global_nodes.description: A significant environmental problem that affects marine ecosystems, including plastic pollution, oil spills, and abandoned fishing gear.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Floating marine debris can harm marine life, contaminate the food chain, and damage coastal ecosystems.

global_nodes.label: Introduction to Physical Oceanography
global_nodes.description: A textbook that provides a comprehensive introduction to physical oceanography, including its principles, processes, and applications.
global_nodes.fun_fact: This textbook is a valuable resource for students and researchers in the field of oceanography.

global_nodes.label: Physical Oceanography of the Transport of Floating Marine Debris
global_nodes.description: A research study that investigates the physical oceanographic processes that transport floating marine debris, including ocean currents, winds, and waves.
global_nodes.fun_fact: This study provides valuable insights into the movement and fate of marine debris in the ocean.

global_nodes.label: Ocean Conservation Efforts
global_nodes.description: A range of practices and policies aimed at protecting the ocean's ecosystems and resources, including marine protected areas, sustainable fishing practices, and reduction of plastic pollution.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ocean conservation efforts can help mitigate the impacts of climate change, overfishing, and pollution on marine ecosystems.

global_nodes.label: Toxicology
global_nodes.description: The study of the adverse effects of chemical substances on living organisms
global_nodes.fun_fact: Toxicology is a multidisciplinary field that overlaps with biology, chemistry, pharmacology, and medicine

global_nodes.label: Pharmacokinetics
global_nodes.description: The study of how an organism affects a specific drug after administration
global_nodes.fun_fact: Pharmacokinetics is crucial in understanding the absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion of drugs

global_nodes.label: Toxicokinetics
global_nodes.description: The study of how a toxic substance is absorbed, distributed, metabolized, and eliminated by an organism
global_nodes.fun_fact: Toxicokinetics helps understand the relationship between dose and effect of toxic substances

global_nodes.label: Ecotoxicology
global_nodes.description: The study of the effects of toxic substances on the environment and ecosystems
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ecotoxicology is essential in understanding the impact of human activities on the environment

global_nodes.label: Risk Assessment
global_nodes.description: The process of evaluating the potential risks associated with a substance or situation
global_nodes.fun_fact: Risk assessment is critical in protecting human health and the environment

global_nodes.label: Regulatory Affairs
global_nodes.description: The process of ensuring compliance with laws and regulations related to toxic substances
global_nodes.fun_fact: Regulatory affairs play a crucial role in protecting public health and the environment

global_nodes.label: Paracelsus
global_nodes.description: A Swiss physician and alchemist who is considered the father of toxicology
global_nodes.fun_fact: Paracelsus introduced the concept of dose-response relationships in toxicology

global_nodes.label: Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors
global_nodes.description: A type of substance that can be used as a pesticide or a pharmaceutical agent
global_nodes.fun_fact: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors can be used to treat diseases such as Alzheimer's and myasthenia gravis

global_nodes.label: Heavy Metal Pollution
global_nodes.description: A type of environmental pollution that can have adverse effects on human health
global_nodes.fun_fact: Heavy metal pollution is a significant problem worldwide, particularly in developing countries

global_nodes.label: Ribosome-Inactivating Proteins
global_nodes.description: A type of protein that can be used as a therapeutic agent to treat cancer
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ribosome-inactivating proteins have shown promise in treating diseases such as leukemia

global_nodes.label: Toxicological Information Extraction
global_nodes.description: The use of computational methods to extract relevant information from large datasets
global_nodes.fun_fact: Toxicological information extraction can help reduce the time and cost associated with manual data analysis

global_nodes.label: Evidence-Based Toxicology
global_nodes.description: An approach that emphasizes the use of empirical evidence to inform toxicological decisions
global_nodes.fun_fact: Evidence-based toxicology is essential in ensuring that toxicological decisions are based on the best available evidence

global_nodes.label: Cancer Research
global_nodes.description: The use of toxicology to understand the mechanisms of cancer and develop new treatments
global_nodes.fun_fact: Toxicology has contributed significantly to our understanding of cancer and the development of new treatments

global_nodes.label: Environmental Pollution
global_nodes.description: The release of toxic substances into the environment, which can have adverse effects on human health and ecosystems
global_nodes.fun_fact: Environmental pollution is a significant problem worldwide, particularly in developing countries

global_nodes.label: Toxicological Testing
global_nodes.description: The use of laboratory tests to evaluate the toxicity of substances
global_nodes.fun_fact: Toxicological testing is essential in ensuring the safety of substances used in consumer products and pharmaceuticals

global_nodes.label: Ethology
global_nodes.description: The scientific study of animal behavior, focusing on the interactions between animals and their environment.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The term 'ethology' was first coined by Isidore Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire in 1859.

global_nodes.label: Animal Behavior
global_nodes.description: The study of the actions and reactions of animals in response to their environment and internal states.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Animal behavior is shaped by a combination of genetic and environmental factors.

global_nodes.label: Social Learning
global_nodes.description: The process by which animals learn new behaviors by observing and imitating others.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Social learning is a key component of cultural transmission in some animal species.

global_nodes.label: Communication
global_nodes.description: The exchange of information between animals, which can take many forms, including vocalizations, visual displays, and chemical signals.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Some animals, like dolphins and primates, possess complex communication systems that rival those of humans.

global_nodes.label: Mating and Courtship
global_nodes.description: The behaviors and rituals associated with mate selection and reproduction.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Some animal species exhibit elaborate courtship displays, such as the peacock's tail feathers.

global_nodes.label: Agonistic Behavior
global_nodes.description: The study of aggressive and competitive behaviors in animals, including fighting, dominance, and submission.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Agonistic behavior can serve as a means of establishing social hierarchy and resolving conflicts.

global_nodes.label: Charles Darwin
global_nodes.description: A British naturalist who laid the foundations for modern ethology with his work on evolution and animal behavior.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Darwin's book 'On the Origin of Species' (1859) revolutionized the field of biology.

global_nodes.label: Nikolaas Tinbergen
global_nodes.description: A Dutch biologist who, along with Konrad Lorenz and Karl von Frisch, was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1973 for his work on animal behavior.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Tinbergen's work on instinct and learning in animals helped establish ethology as a distinct field.

global_nodes.label: Konrad Lorenz
global_nodes.description: An Austrian biologist who made significant contributions to the field of ethology, including the study of imprinting and social behavior in animals.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Lorenz was a key figure in the development of modern ethology and was known for his charismatic personality and writing style.

global_nodes.label: Karl von Frisch
global_nodes.description: An Austrian biologist who was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1973 for his work on the behavior and social organization of honeybees.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Von Frisch's research on bee communication and navigation helped establish the importance of social learning in insects.

global_nodes.label: Neuroanatomy
global_nodes.description: The study of the structure and organization of the nervous system, which underlies animal behavior.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Understanding neuroanatomy is essential for explaining the neural basis of behavior.

global_nodes.label: Ecology
global_nodes.description: The study of the interactions between organisms and their environment, which is closely related to ethology.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ecological principles can help explain the evolution of animal behavior and the distribution of species.

global_nodes.label: Evolutionary Biology
global_nodes.description: The study of the processes that have shaped the diversity of life on Earth, including the evolution of animal behavior.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Evolutionary biology provides a framework for understanding the adaptive significance of animal behavior.

global_nodes.label: Animal Training
global_nodes.description: The practical application of ethological principles to train animals for various purposes, such as companionship, assistance, or conservation.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Animal training relies on a deep understanding of animal behavior and learning processes.

global_nodes.label: Conservation Biology
global_nodes.description: The application of ethological principles to conserve and manage animal populations, including the development of effective conservation strategies.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Conservation biology relies on a thorough understanding of animal behavior and ecology to protect endangered species.

global_nodes.label: Hydrology
global_nodes.description: The scientific study of the movement, distribution, and management of water on Earth and other planets.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The word 'hydrology' comes from the Greek words 'hydor' meaning water and 'logos' meaning study.

global_nodes.label: Water Cycle
global_nodes.description: The continuous process by which water is circulated between the Earth and the atmosphere.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The water cycle is also known as the hydrologic cycle.

global_nodes.label: Groundwater Flow
global_nodes.description: The movement of water beneath the Earth's surface.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Groundwater flow is an important component of the water cycle.

global_nodes.label: Surface Water Runoff
global_nodes.description: The flow of water over the Earth's surface.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Surface water runoff can lead to flooding and erosion.

global_nodes.label: Watershed Management
global_nodes.description: The practice of managing and protecting watersheds to maintain water quality and prevent flooding.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Watershed management involves a combination of technical, social, and economic approaches.

global_nodes.label: Flood Control
global_nodes.description: The practice of preventing or mitigating the effects of flooding.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Flood control measures can include dams, levees, and floodplain management.

global_nodes.label: Hydrologist
global_nodes.description: A scientist who studies the movement, distribution, and management of water on Earth and other planets.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Hydrologists can work in a variety of fields, including environmental science, civil engineering, and physical geography.

global_nodes.label: Statistics of Extremes
global_nodes.description: A statistical approach to understanding extreme hydrological events, such as floods and droughts.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Statistics of extremes is used to predict and prepare for rare but potentially catastrophic events.

global_nodes.label: Physically Based Modeling
global_nodes.description: A modeling approach that uses physical principles to simulate hydrological processes.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Physically based modeling is used to predict water flow, sediment transport, and other hydrological processes.

global_nodes.label: Twenty-three Unsolved Problems in Hydrology
global_nodes.description: A community initiative to identify and address major unsolved scientific problems in hydrology.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The initiative aims to harmonize research efforts and advance the field of hydrology.

global_nodes.label: Water Resources Management
global_nodes.description: The practice of managing and conserving water resources to meet human needs and protect the environment.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Water resources management involves a combination of technical, social, and economic approaches.

global_nodes.label: Drainage Basin Sustainability
global_nodes.description: The ability of a drainage basin to maintain its natural processes and functions over time.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Drainage basin sustainability is critical for maintaining water quality and preventing flooding.

global_nodes.label: Channel Network Topology
global_nodes.description: The study of the geometric and topological properties of channel networks.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Channel network topology is used to understand and predict water flow and sediment transport.

global_nodes.label: Dynamic Contributing Areas
global_nodes.description: The areas that contribute to runoff and flooding in a given watershed.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Dynamic contributing areas are used to predict and prepare for flooding and erosion.

global_nodes.label: Hydrological Forecasting
global_nodes.description: The practice of predicting future hydrological events, such as floods and droughts.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Hydrological forecasting is used to inform decision-making and mitigate the impacts of extreme events.

global_nodes.label: Food Security
global_nodes.description: The state of having reliable access to a sufficient quantity of affordable, healthy food.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The concept of food security was first introduced in 1974 at the World Food Conference.

global_nodes.label: Agricultural Economics
global_nodes.description: The study of the production, distribution, and consumption of agricultural products.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Agricultural economics is a key factor in ensuring food security, as it helps to optimize crop yields and reduce waste.

global_nodes.label: Nutrition Science
global_nodes.description: The study of the relationship between food and human health.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Nutrition science plays a critical role in ensuring that food is not only available, but also nutritious and safe to eat.

global_nodes.label: Sustainable Agriculture
global_nodes.description: The practice of farming in a way that minimizes environmental impact and promotes long-term sustainability.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Sustainable agriculture is essential for maintaining soil health, conserving water, and reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

global_nodes.label: Climate Resilience
global_nodes.description: The ability of agricultural systems to withstand and recover from climate-related shocks and stresses.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Climate resilience is critical for ensuring that food systems can adapt to the impacts of climate change, such as droughts and floods.

global_nodes.label: Global Food Systems
global_nodes.description: The complex network of systems that produce, process, distribute, and consume food globally.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Global food systems are influenced by a range of factors, including trade policies, transportation infrastructure, and consumer demand.

global_nodes.label: Precision Agriculture
global_nodes.description: The use of advanced technologies, such as drones and satellite imaging, to optimize crop yields and reduce waste.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Precision agriculture can help farmers to reduce their environmental impact while increasing productivity and efficiency.

global_nodes.label: Food Insecurity
global_nodes.description: The state of being without reliable access to a sufficient quantity of affordable, healthy food.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Food insecurity affects over 820 million people worldwide, with the majority living in developing countries.

global_nodes.label: Climate Change
global_nodes.description: The long-term warming of the planet due to human activities, such as burning fossil fuels and deforestation.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Climate change is a major threat to food security, as it can lead to more frequent and severe weather events, such as droughts and floods.

global_nodes.label: Norman Borlaug
global_nodes.description: An American agronomist who developed high-yielding, disease-resistant wheat varieties that helped to increase food production in developing countries.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Norman Borlaug was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1970 for his contributions to food security and world peace.

global_nodes.label: Green Revolution
global_nodes.description: A period of rapid increase in food production in the 1960s and 1970s, driven by the introduction of high-yielding crop varieties and irrigation systems.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The Green Revolution helped to reduce hunger and poverty in many developing countries, but also had negative environmental impacts, such as soil degradation and water pollution.

global_nodes.label: FAO
global_nodes.description: The Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, which works to reduce hunger and poverty by promoting sustainable agriculture and food security.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The FAO was established in 1945 and has 194 member countries, making it one of the largest and most diverse organizations in the world.

global_nodes.label: Sustainable Development Goals
global_nodes.description: A set of 17 goals adopted by the United Nations in 2015, which aim to end poverty, protect the planet, and ensure peace and prosperity for all.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The Sustainable Development Goals include a specific goal on food security (Goal 2), which aims to end hunger and all forms of malnutrition by 2030.

global_nodes.label: Agroecology
global_nodes.description: An approach to agriculture that emphasizes the use of ecological principles to promote sustainable and resilient food systems.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Agroecology can help to promote biodiversity, improve soil health, and reduce the use of external inputs, such as fertilizers and pesticides.

global_nodes.label: Food Waste
global_nodes.description: The loss or discard of food that is safe and nutritious for human consumption.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Food waste is a major problem globally, with an estimated one-third of all food produced being lost or wasted.

global_nodes.label: Archaeomagnetism
global_nodes.description: The study of the Earth's magnetic field as recorded in archaeological materials
global_nodes.fun_fact: Archaeomagnetism was first conceived by Émile Thellier in the 1930s

global_nodes.label: Geomagnetic Reversals
global_nodes.description: The process by which the Earth's magnetic field reverses its polarity
global_nodes.fun_fact: Geomagnetic reversals have occurred many times in the Earth's history

global_nodes.label: Paleomagnetism
global_nodes.description: The study of the Earth's magnetic field as recorded in rocks and sediments
global_nodes.fun_fact: Paleomagnetism is used to reconstruct the Earth's magnetic field in the past

global_nodes.label: Magnetic Dating
global_nodes.description: The use of archaeomagnetism to date archaeological sites
global_nodes.fun_fact: Magnetic dating is used to date sites within the last 10,000 years

global_nodes.label: Geophysical Surveying
global_nodes.description: The use of geophysical techniques to survey and map the subsurface
global_nodes.fun_fact: Geophysical surveying is used to locate and map archaeological sites

global_nodes.label: Geoarchaeology
global_nodes.description: The application of geological techniques to archaeological research
global_nodes.fun_fact: Geoarchaeology is used to reconstruct the environmental and geological context of archaeological sites

global_nodes.label: Émile Thellier
global_nodes.description: The French geophysicist who first conceived the idea of archaeomagnetism
global_nodes.fun_fact: Thellier's work laid the foundation for the development of archaeomagnetism as a dating technique

global_nodes.label: SQUID Magnetometers
global_nodes.description: Highly sensitive magnetometers used to measure the magnetic properties of materials
global_nodes.fun_fact: SQUID magnetometers have greatly improved the accuracy and precision of archaeomagnetic dating

global_nodes.label: Italian Archaeomagnetic Data
global_nodes.description: A compilation of archaeomagnetic data from Italy, including new results and a preliminary secular variation curve
global_nodes.fun_fact: The Italian data set includes results from volcanic deposits of unquestionable age

global_nodes.label: Hungarian Archaeomagnetic Database
global_nodes.description: A database of archaeomagnetic directions from Hungary, with age estimates falling between BC 350 and AD 1800
global_nodes.fun_fact: The Hungarian database has been increased by the addition of 60 new directional results

global_nodes.label: British Directional Database
global_nodes.description: A database of archaeomagnetic directions from Britain, with age estimates falling between BC 2000 and AD 2000
global_nodes.fun_fact: The British database includes results from 466 archaeological sites

global_nodes.label: Radiometric Dating
global_nodes.description: A technique used to date materials by measuring the decay of radioactive isotopes
global_nodes.fun_fact: Radiometric dating is often used in conjunction with archaeomagnetism to date archaeological sites

global_nodes.label: Geomagnetic Polarity Time Scale
global_nodes.description: A record of the Earth's magnetic field polarity over time
global_nodes.fun_fact: The geomagnetic polarity time scale is used to constrain the age of materials

global_nodes.label: Ferromagnetic Materials
global_nodes.description: Materials that exhibit magnetic properties, such as magnetite
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ferromagnetic materials are used to record the Earth's magnetic field in archaeological materials

global_nodes.label: Curie Point
global_nodes.description: The temperature below which a material becomes ferromagnetic
global_nodes.fun_fact: The Curie point is important in archaeomagnetism as it determines when a material becomes magnetized

global_nodes.label: Anthropology
global_nodes.description: The scientific study of humanity, encompassing biology, sociology, and culture.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The term 'anthropology' was first used in the 16th century.

global_nodes.label: Cultural Anthropology
global_nodes.description: The study of cultural meaning, including norms, values, and practices.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Cultural anthropology emerged as a distinct field in the early 20th century.

global_nodes.label: Linguistic Anthropology
global_nodes.description: The study of language and its influence on social life.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Linguistic anthropology has shed light on the diversity of human languages.

global_nodes.label: Archaeological Anthropology
global_nodes.description: The study of past human cultures through material remains.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Archaeological anthropology has uncovered the secrets of ancient civilizations.

global_nodes.label: Biological Anthropology
global_nodes.description: The study of human biology and evolution.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Biological anthropology has revealed the origins of the human species.

global_nodes.label: Applied Anthropology
global_nodes.description: The practical application of anthropological knowledge to real-world problems.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Applied anthropology has improved healthcare, education, and economic development in many communities.

global_nodes.label: Franz Boas
global_nodes.description: A German-American anthropologist who founded the American Anthropological Association.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Boas is considered one of the founders of modern anthropology.

global_nodes.label: Margaret Mead
global_nodes.description: An American anthropologist who studied cultural differences and their impact on human behavior.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Mead's work on adolescence in different cultures remains influential to this day.

global_nodes.label: Human Evolution
global_nodes.description: The study of the biological and cultural development of the human species.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Human evolution is a complex and still-unfolding process.

global_nodes.label: Cultural Relativism
global_nodes.description: The idea that cultural practices and values should be understood within their own context.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Cultural relativism has helped anthropologists avoid ethnocentric biases.

global_nodes.label: Ethnography
global_nodes.description: A research methodology that involves long-term immersion in a culture to gain in-depth understanding.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ethnography has been used to study a wide range of cultures, from indigenous communities to urban gangs.

global_nodes.label: Forensic Anthropology
global_nodes.description: The application of anthropological knowledge to aid in the identification of human remains.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Forensic anthropology has helped solve crimes and identify victims of natural disasters.

global_nodes.label: Medical Anthropology
global_nodes.description: The study of the cultural and social factors that influence health and healthcare.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Medical anthropology has improved our understanding of the social determinants of health.

global_nodes.label: Anthropological Theory
global_nodes.description: The development of theoretical frameworks to explain human culture and behavior.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Anthropological theory has been influenced by a range of disciplines, including sociology, psychology, and philosophy.

global_nodes.label: Anthropology and Policy
global_nodes.description: The application of anthropological knowledge to inform policy decisions.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Anthropology has been used to inform policy on issues such as immigration, education, and environmental conservation.

global_nodes.label: Biodiversity
global_nodes.description: The variability of life on Earth, including genetic, species, ecosystem, and phylogenetic diversity.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The term 'biodiversity' was coined in 1985 by biologist Walter G. Rosen.

global_nodes.label: Species Extinction
global_nodes.description: The process by which a species becomes extinct, often due to human activities such as habitat destruction and climate change.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The current rate of species extinction is estimated to be 100-1,000 times higher than the natural rate.

global_nodes.label: Ecosystem Services
global_nodes.description: The benefits that humans derive from functioning ecosystems, including clean air and water, soil formation, and climate regulation.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ecosystem services are estimated to be worth over $33 trillion annually, which is more than the global GDP.

global_nodes.label: Conservation Biology
global_nodes.description: The scientific study of the conservation of species and ecosystems, with the goal of preserving biodiversity and maintaining ecosystem services.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Conservation biology is an interdisciplinary field that combines biology, ecology, economics, and social sciences.

global_nodes.label: Taxonomy
global_nodes.description: The science of classifying and naming living organisms, which is essential for understanding biodiversity and conservation.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The most widely used taxonomic system is the Linnaean system, developed by Carl Linnaeus in the 18th century.

global_nodes.label: Ecological Restoration
global_nodes.description: The process of restoring degraded or damaged ecosystems to a healthy and functional state, which can help to preserve biodiversity and ecosystem services.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ecological restoration can be used to restore a variety of ecosystems, including wetlands, forests, and grasslands.

global_nodes.label: Tropical Forests
global_nodes.description: Ecosystems found near the equator, which are home to a vast array of plant and animal species and provide important ecosystem services.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Tropical forests cover less than 10% of the Earth's surface but are home to over 50% of all species.

global_nodes.label: Latitudinal Gradients
global_nodes.description: The patterns of species diversity and distribution that vary with latitude, with the tropics generally having higher species diversity than temperate regions.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Latitudinal gradients are influenced by a variety of factors, including climate, geography, and evolutionary history.

global_nodes.label: Biodiversity Hotspots
global_nodes.description: Regions with exceptionally high levels of endemism and species diversity, which are often prioritized for conservation efforts.
global_nodes.fun_fact: There are currently 36 recognized biodiversity hotspots around the world, which cover only 2.3% of the Earth's surface but are home to over 50% of all plant species and 42% of all terrestrial vertebrate species.

global_nodes.label: Walter G. Rosen
global_nodes.description: An American biologist who coined the term 'biodiversity' in 1985 and played a key role in promoting the conservation of biodiversity.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Walter G. Rosen was a prominent figure in the development of the field of conservation biology.

global_nodes.label: Food Webs
global_nodes.description: The networks of relationships between species that feed on each other, which are essential for understanding ecosystem functioning and biodiversity.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Food webs can be complex and involve many different species, making them challenging to study and model.

global_nodes.label: Ecosystem Functioning
global_nodes.description: The processes that occur within ecosystems, including primary production, decomposition, and nutrient cycling, which are essential for maintaining ecosystem services.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Ecosystem functioning is influenced by a variety of factors, including climate, geography, and species composition.

global_nodes.label: Human Water Security
global_nodes.description: The availability of sufficient and safe water for human consumption, which is often threatened by human activities such as pollution and over-extraction.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Human water security is closely linked to biodiversity and ecosystem services, as healthy ecosystems are essential for maintaining water quality and quantity.

global_nodes.label: River Biodiversity
global_nodes.description: The variety of plant and animal species found in rivers, which are often threatened by human activities such as damming and pollution.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Rivers are home to a wide variety of species, including fish, amphibians, and birds, and play a critical role in maintaining ecosystem services.

global_nodes.label: Climate Change
global_nodes.description: The long-term warming of the planet due to human activities such as burning fossil fuels and deforestation, which is a major threat to biodiversity and ecosystem services.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Climate change is projected to have significant impacts on biodiversity and ecosystem services, including changes in species distribution and extinction risk.

global_nodes.label: Soil Science
global_nodes.description: The study of soil as a natural resource, including its formation, classification, and properties.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Soil science is a multidisciplinary field that draws from biology, chemistry, physics, and geology.

global_nodes.label: Pedology
global_nodes.description: The study of the formation, properties, and classification of soils.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The term 'pedology' comes from the Greek word 'pedon,' meaning soil or earth.

global_nodes.label: Edaphology
global_nodes.description: The study of the relationship between soils and plants, including soil fertility and plant nutrition.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Edaphology is essential for understanding how to optimize crop yields and promote sustainable agriculture.

global_nodes.label: Soil Erosion
global_nodes.description: The process of soil being worn away or removed by natural or human-induced factors, such as wind or water.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Soil erosion can lead to significant economic and environmental losses, including decreased crop yields and increased sedimentation in waterways.

global_nodes.label: Soil Conservation
global_nodes.description: The practice of protecting and preserving soil resources through sustainable land management techniques.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Soil conservation is critical for maintaining soil health, preventing erosion, and supporting biodiversity.

global_nodes.label: Soil Fertility
global_nodes.description: The ability of soil to support plant growth and provide essential nutrients.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Soil fertility is influenced by factors such as soil pH, nutrient availability, and microbial activity.

global_nodes.label: Hiltner
global_nodes.description: A German scientist who recognized the importance of the rhizosphere in soil science.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Hiltner's work laid the foundation for modern understanding of soil-plant interactions.

global_nodes.label: Rhizosphere
global_nodes.description: The region of soil surrounding plant roots, where complex interactions between plants, microorganisms, and soil occur.
global_nodes.fun_fact: The rhizosphere is a critical zone for nutrient cycling and plant nutrition.

global_nodes.label: Sustainable Development
global_nodes.description: The practice of meeting present needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Soil science plays a vital role in achieving sustainable development goals, such as food security and environmental conservation.

global_nodes.label: UN Sustainable Development Goals
global_nodes.description: A set of 17 global goals adopted by the United Nations in 2015, aiming to promote sustainable development and address pressing global challenges.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Soil science contributes to several UN SDGs, including Zero Hunger, Clean Water and Sanitation, and Life on Land.

global_nodes.label: Electrical Resistivity
global_nodes.description: A non-invasive technique used to measure the electrical properties of soils and assess their composition and structure.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Electrical resistivity surveys can help identify soil contaminants, map soil layers, and monitor soil moisture.

global_nodes.label: Soil Mapping
global_nodes.description: The process of creating detailed maps of soil types, properties, and distributions.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Soil mapping is essential for land use planning, natural resource management, and environmental monitoring.

global_nodes.label: Precision Agriculture
global_nodes.description: The use of advanced technologies, such as GPS and drones, to optimize crop yields, reduce waste, and promote sustainable agriculture.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Precision agriculture relies heavily on soil science and spatial analysis to make data-driven decisions.

global_nodes.label: Soil Biology
global_nodes.description: The study of the living organisms in soil, including microorganisms, plants, and animals.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Soil biology plays a critical role in ecosystem functioning, nutrient cycling, and soil health.

global_nodes.label: Soil Chemistry
global_nodes.description: The study of the chemical properties and processes that occur in soils.
global_nodes.fun_fact: Soil chemistry is essential for understanding soil fertility, nutrient availability, and environmental pollution.
